en.extract:
The Nicobar sparrowhawk (Tachyspiza butleri) is a species of bird of prey in the family Accipitridae. It is endemic to the Nicobar Islands of India. There are two subspecies, the nominate race which is found on Car Nicobar in the north of the archipelago, and T. b. obsoleta, from Katchal and Camorta in the central part of the Nicobars. A museum specimen originally attributed to this species from the island of Great Nicobar was later found to be a misidentified Besra. This species was formerly classified in the genus Accipiter.
Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests. It is threatened by habitat loss.


Description
It has a length of 28 to 34 cm and a wingspan of 50 to 57 cm.


References


External links

BirdLife Species Factsheet.

The '''Nicobar sparrowhawk'''<!--, '''Épervier Des Nicobar''', or '''Gavilán De Nicobar'''--> ('''''Tachyspiza butleri''''') is a species of [[bird of prey]] in the family [[Accipitridae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to the [[Nicobar Islands]] of [[India]]. There are two subspecies, the nominate race which is found on [[Car Nicobar]] in the north of the archipelago, and ''T. b. obsoleta'', from [[Katchal Island|Katchal]] and [[Camorta Island|Camorta]] in the central part of the Nicobars. A [[bird collections|museum specimen]] originally attributed to this species from the island of [[Great Nicobar]] was later found to be a misidentified [[Besra]].<ref>{{cite journal|last=Rasmussen|first=Pamela|title=On the status of the Nicobar Sparrowhawk ''Accipiter butleri'' on Great Nicobar Island, India|journal=Forktail|year=2000|volume=16|page=185|url=http://www.orientalbirdclub.org/publications/forktail/16pdfs/Rasmussen-Sparrowhawk.pdf|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110610155408/http://www.orientalbirdclub.org/publications/forktail/16pdfs/Rasmussen-Sparrowhawk.pdf|archive-date=2011-06-10}}</ref> This species was formerly classified in the genus ''[[Accipiter]]''.

Its natural [[habitat]] is subtropical or tropical moist lowland [[forest]]s. It is threatened by [[habitat loss]].

== Description ==
It has a length of 28 to 34 cm and a wingspan of 50 to 57 cm.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Ferguson-Lees |first=James |title=Raptors of the World |date=2005 |publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing Plc |others=David A. Christie |isbn=978-0-7136-6957-2 |series=Helm Field Guides |location=London}}</ref>
